title: The Gene: An Intimate History
awards: Pulitzer Prize for General Nonfiction (2011) — for The Emperor of All Maladies
PEN/E.O. Wilson Literary Science Writing Award — Finalist (2017)
Royal Society Science Book Prize — Shortlisted (2017)
New York Times Top 10 Best Books of 2016
themes: The history of gene discovery from Mendel to CRISPR
Heredity and the search for the fundamental unit of life
The intertwining of science and human identity
The legacy of eugenics and its unsettling lessons
The political, ethical, and medical power of genetic knowledge
Cancer as a disease of the genome
The decoding of the human genome
CRISPR and the future of genetic engineering
Mental illness, schizophrenia, and genetic vulnerability
The Mukherjee family's intimate encounter with genetic destiny
Ethical dilemmas of editing the code of human life
What does it mean to know your genetic future?
excerpt: A magisterial, deeply human history of the gene — from a 19th-century monastery garden to the CRISPR laboratories of the 21st century — and what it means for the future of our species.
authorBiography: Siddhartha Mukherjee is an Indian-American physician, oncologist, cell biologist, and writer. Born in 1970 in New Delhi, India, he completed his undergraduate studies at Stanford University, earned his DPhil in cancer genetics from Oxford as a Rhodes Scholar, and received his MD from Harvard Medical School. He is an assistant professor of medicine at Columbia University and a staff cancer physician at NewYork-Presbyterian Hospital. His first book, The Emperor of All Maladies: A Biography of Cancer, won the Pulitzer Prize for General Nonfiction in 2011 and was named one of Time's all-time 100 best nonfiction books. He lives in New York City with his wife, the artist Sarah Sze, and their two daughters.
authorKnownFor: The Emperor of All Maladies: A Biography of Cancer (2010)
The Song of the Cell: An Exploration of Medicine and the New Human (2022)
Oncologist and cell biologist at Columbia University Medical Center
